coredata.dc:title: Spray-coated monodispersed SnO2 microsphere films as scaffold layers for efficient mesoscopic perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Xinyi
Rui, Yichuan
Han, Xuefei
Yang, Jingxia
Wang, Yuanqiang
Zhang, Qinghong
coredata.dc:description: Mesoporous SnO2 microspheres are a class of promising electron transport materials for mesoscopic perovskite solar cells, but a smooth and dense film as requested by electron transport layer is difficult to realize using the microspheres. Here, a series of monodispersed SnO2 microspheres with high specific surface area and good crystallinity are synthesized via a surfactant-free solvothermal method. These SnO2 microspheres showing small diameters of about 75, 110 and 200 nm with narrow size distribution are achieved by precisely controlling the crystal growth process. They are suitable for constructing the electron transport layers whose thickness is only about hundreds nanometers. Besides, as the microspheres easily slip off from the substrate during the conventional spin coating process, the spray coating method is exploited to prepare high quality electron transport layers. As a result, a power conversion efficiency of 16.85% is yielded by using the 75 nm sized SnO2 microspheres as the electron transport layers. This high power conversion efficiency is attributed to the fast electron transport and inhibited charge recombination. To further improve the charge transfer between the electron transport layer and perovskite layer, graphene quantum dots are added into the SnO2 microspheres, and a best efficiency of 17.08% is obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Monodispersed SnO2 microspheres
Electron transport layer
Perovskite solar cells
Spray coating

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electron transport layer preparation and solar cell assembly

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of the homogenous SnO2 microspheres: (a, b) HS-A, (c, d) HS-B, (e, f) HS-C.
TEM and HRTEM images the SnO2 microspheres: (a) HS-A, (b) HS-B, (c, d) HS-C. The inset shows the SAED pattern of HS-C.
(a) XRD patterns (b) nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms (c) typical XPS survey and (d) high-resolution Sn 3d spectra of the HS-A, HS-B and HS-C microspheres.

(a) Surface SEM image of HS-C electron transport layer prepared by spin coating. (b, c) Surface and (d) cross-sectional SEM images of HS-C electron transport layer prepared by spray coating. (e) Schematic diagram of spray coating process and the effect of spraying time on the surface morphology evolution. The red dotted circle shows the best state. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Transmittance spectra of the SnO2 microspheres based ETLs and a reference ETL. The inset shows the digital photo of the HS-C microsphere film on the FTO substrate. (b) UV–Vis absorbance spectra of FTO/Ref/perovskite, FTO/HS-A/perovskite, FTO/HS-B/perovskite and FTO/HS-C/perovskite samples, respectively. (c) Surface morphology of the perovskite layer atop of the HS-C microsphere film. (d) Cross-sectional SEM image of the completed perovskite solar cell.

(a) Current–voltage curves and (b) IPCE spectra of the PSCs based on different SnO2 ETLs. Statistics of the photovoltaic parameters (c) Jsc, (d) Voc, (e) FF, (f) PCE of PSCs based on HS-A, HS-B, HS-C and Ref ETL.
Nyquist plots of the PSCs based on different SnO2 ETLs probed at open circuit voltage under illumination, and the inset is the equivalent circuit.
J-V curve of the PSC prepared from graphene quantum dots modified HS-C SnO2 microspheres.
Photovoltaic parameters of the perovskite solar cells based on Ref, HS-A, HS-B and HS-C ETLs.

head.ce:title: Spray-coated monodispersed SnO2 microsphere films as scaffold layers for efficient mesoscopic perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University of Engineering Science, Shanghai, 201620, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University of Engineering Science, Shanghai, 201620, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai, 201620, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai, 201620, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mesoporous SnO2 microspheres are a class of promising electron transport materials for mesoscopic perovskite solar cells, but a smooth and dense film as requested by electron transport layer is difficult to realize using the microspheres. Here, a series of monodispersed SnO2 microspheres with high specific surface area and good crystallinity are synthesized via a surfactant-free solvothermal method. These SnO2 microspheres showing small diameters of about 75, 110 and 200 nm with narrow size distribution are achieved by precisely controlling the crystal growth process. They are suitable for constructing the electron transport layers whose thickness is only about hundreds nanometers. Besides, as the microspheres easily slip off from the substrate during the conventional spin coating process, the spray coating method is exploited to prepare high quality electron transport layers. As a result, a power conversion efficiency of 16.85% is yielded by using the 75 nm sized SnO2 microspheres as the electron transport layers. This high power conversion efficiency is attributed to the fast electron transport and inhibited charge recombination. To further improve the charge transfer between the electron transport layer and perovskite layer, graphene quantum dots are added into the SnO2 microspheres, and a best efficiency of 17.08% is obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic-inorganic halide perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have been considered as one of the most promising candidates for the next generation solar cells. The impressive photovoltaic performance of PSCs originates from the outstanding optical and electronic characteristics of the organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite including high light absorption, low exciton binding energy and long carrier diffusion length [1–3]. The sandwich structure inside a PSC consists of an electron transport layer (ETL), a perovskite light-absorbing layer and a hole transport layer (HTL) [4,5]. The electron transport layer selectively extracts electrons, playing a key role in making effective contact with the perovskite light absorber [6]. Generally, PSCs without configuring ETLs are found to have very poor performance than those with ETLs [7,8]. ETL components should not be neglected in the latest developing stage of perovskite solar cells.

State-of-the-art mesoscopic PSCs commonly use TiO2 nanoparticles as the electron transport material [9]. However, a high temperature annealing process is necessary to remove the organic adhesive which obviously restricts the application in flexible devices. As an important intrinsic n-type wide bandgap semiconductor, SnO2 has received attractive attentions due to the extraordinary advantage of high electron mobility (240 cm2 V−1 s−1) [10]. Besides, the bandgap of SnO2 (3.6–3.8 eV) is wider than TiO2 (3.0–3.2 eV), which helps to enhance the UV resistance of perovskite due to the low sensitivity to ultraviolet radiation [11]. Hence, SnO2 has been considered as a promising alternative to traditional TiO2 [12,13]. Recently, TiO2 microspheres have been explored as the mesoporous electron transport layers in PSCs [14]. The microsphere constructed mesoporous layer has sizable external pores which will facilitate the deposition of CH3NH3PbI3. Also, the spherical nanostructure generates a large surface area, which will be an advantage for efficient charge injection from the perovskite to the ETL conduction band. Furthermore, the tight connection between the microspheres could enhance the efficiency of electron transfer. SnO2 microspheres have been used as the photoanodes in dye-sensitized solar cells, but rarely in perovskite solar cells [15,16]. Because the previous reported SnO2 microspheres have a diameter large than hundreds of nanometers, which are unable to fabricate the electron transport layer whose ideal thickness should be around 200 nm [17]. Besides, the dimension, shape and distribution of SnO2 microspheres also influence the pore structure as well as the connectivity among the spheres. The inhomogeneous spheres lead to an undesirable pores size and shape distributions. Thus far, fabricating SnO2 microspheres with homogeneous diameters, small size but high crystallinity is still a challenge.

The conventional deposition method that used to prepare the electron transport layer is spin coating. However, the coverage of the microsphere film is not satisfied, as the microspheres are easy to slip off from the fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) substrates [14]. The exposed area without covering microspheres would cause short-circuit contact between the perovskite and the FTO, which subsequently aggravates the charge recombination. Spin coating method not only wastes raw material but also is unable to fabricate large area films [18]. To realize high quality films and economically scalable process, alternative deposition techniques are highly demanded [19]. In the field of PSCs, the spray coating method has been used to prepare conductive semitransparent layers [20], compact blocking layers [21], and perovskite layers [22]. In the early stage, Kim et al. used spray coating to prepare the mesoporous TiO2 electron-collection layer and obtained a PCE over 15% [18]. This PCE benefits from the advantages of spray coating such as the uniform film coverage, the ability to provide an easily controllable directional deposition. Besides, it has a good capability to a variety of substrates, making this method potential to the deposition of continuous layers [23]. Simply, by adjusting the spray coating time, it is convenient to achieve a fine film thickness from tens to hundreds of nanometers and even micrometers [24,25].

This study reports the synthesis of monodispersed SnO2 microspheres with tunable size and fine crystallinity by using a surfactant-free solvothermal method. A series of homogeneous SnO2 microspheres with diameters from 75 to 200 nm are obtained. In order to realize a uniform electron transport film of PSCs, the spray coating method is adopted to deposit the SnO2 microspheres. In addition, we systematically investigate the effects of sphere size on the photovoltaic performance of perovskite solar cells.

Unless otherwise specified, all materials and reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. NH2CHNH2I, CH3NH3Br, Lead iodide (PbI2, 99%), lead bromide (PbBr2, 99%) and Spiro-OMeTAD (purity≥99.5%) were purchased from Xi'an Polymer Light Technology Corp. Lithium Bis (trifluoromethane) sulfonimide (Li-TFSI) and 4-tert-butylpyridine (t-BP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received.

Tin (II) chloride dihydrate (SnCl2·2H2O) was dissolved in a premixed solution consisting of 50 mL n-propanol and 1.5 mL concentrated hydrochloric acid. After ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min, magnetic stirring was conducted for 60 min until the solution became transparent. The solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave with 80 mL capacity for further subjected to solvothermal treatment. All the synthetic parameters as the amount of tin precursor, the temperature and duration of solvothermal treatment would influence the morphologies of the SnO2 samples, so the products prepared under different conditions were investigated as listed in Table S1, and the corresponding SEM images are shown in Fig. S1. Three kinds of homogenous SnO2 microspheres with different diameters were chose as the objects for further study, which were synthesized under the conditions of 2 mmol of SnCl2·2H2O and solvothermal treated at 200 °C for 12 h (named as HS-A), 1 mmol of SnCl2·2H2O and treated at 180 °C for 6 h (HS-B), and 2 mmol of SnCl2·2H2O and treated at 180 °C for 6 h (HS-C), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron transport layer preparation and solar cell assembly
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared SnO2 microspheres were thoroughly washed by using deionized water and ethanol, and then re-dispersed into ethanol to prepare the colloids for spray coating. The SnO2 microsphere electron transport layers were attained by spraying the colloids onto the FTO substrates which were preheated on a 100 °C hot plate for 10 min. The diameter of the spray nozzle is 0.22 mm. Then the SnO2 microsphere layers were annealed at 200 °C for 30 min at air atmosphere. For a better comparison, SnO2 nanoparticles synthesized by a solvothermal method according to our previous recipe were also introduced as the electron transport layers [26,27]. The SnO2 nanoparticles are well crystallized with the particle sizes of about 15 nm (Fig. S2). The nanoparticle electron transport layer was prepared by spin coating with 3000 rpm for 30 s, and named as Ref. The precursor solution of (FAPbI3)0.85(MAPbBr3)0.15 perovskite was prepared in a glovebox. 344 mg NH2CHNH2I (1.0 M), 1014 mg PbI2 (1.1 M), 44.8 mg CH3NH3Br (0.2 M) and 146.8 mg PbBr2 (0.2 M) powders were dissolved into a mixed solvent of DMF (1.6 mL) and DMSO (0.4 mL). The precursor solution of perovskite was deposited onto the ETLs by spin coating at 1000 rpm for 10 s and 6000 rpm for 20s, and 270 μL of chloroform was poured onto the substrate during the last 10 s. The films were then annealed on a hot plate at 100 °C for 20 min. After cooling down to room temperature, spin coating with 4000 rpm was conducted for 10 s to cover the prepared perovskite film with Spiro-OMeTAD solution. Finally, to pattern the electrodes, the gold electrodes with a thickness of about 60 nm were prepared through an aperture mask by using a thermal evaporator.

The phase of SnO2 products was characterized by using a Rigaku D/Max 2550 V X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα (λ = 0.154 nm) radiation. The micro-morphologies of the SnO2 microspheres and devices were observed by using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, SU-8010, Hitachi, Japan). To further confirm the intrinsic structure, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns were obtained by using a JEM 2100F microscope (JEOL Co., Japan). The specific surface areas of the samples were obtained by using a Micromeritics ASAP-2020 surface area instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected on Escalab 250 Xi. The transmittance and absorption spectra of ETLs and perovskite films were collected on an ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) spectra photometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan). Keithley 2400 Source Meter was used to perform the photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) curves under the Newport solar simulator (Model 96160) with the light intensity calibrated to 100 mW cm−2. The incident-photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra were measured as a function of wavelength from 300 to 800 nm by the Newport designed IPCE system. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at open circuit voltage under AM1.5G illumination by using a Zahner-elecktrik (Germany) workstation.

Three kinds of homogeneous SnO2 microspheres with different diameters are obtained by finely adjusting the synthetic parameters as precursor concentration, reaction temperature and duration. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, the homogeneous SnO2 microspheres with the diameters of ~200 nm are obtained at 200 °C for 12 h with 2 mmol Tin (II) chloride dihydrate. Fig. 1c and d shows SnO2 microspheres with decreased diameters of ~110 nm obtained at 180 °C for 6 h with 1 mmol tin precursor. The sizes of the microspheres could further decrease to ~75 nm when the precursor increases to 2 mmol (Fig. 1c, f). Such tiny and homogenous SnO2 microspheres have been rarely reported thus far. Especially, the as-prepared SnO2 microspheres assemble to a uniform hexagonal honeycomb structure due to even size distribution (Fig. 1c and d). Generally, it is difficult to obtain homogeneous SnO2 microspheres due to the high reactivity of tin precursor in the conventional alkaline hydrolysis environment. Herein, an acidic hydrolysis solution is developed where the nucleation and growth rate could decelerate effectively. It makes the primary nanoparticles which further construct the secondary microspheres grow evenly. With the temperature and reaction time decrease, the crystallization rate of the SnO2 microspheres is reduced during the solvothermal process. On the other hand, the increased amount of stannous chloride dihydrate will increase the nucleation sites, resulting in a smaller sphere size. Meanwhile, rapid crystal growth at high temperature could increase the uniformity of the microspheres. However, when the temperature or duration of the solvothermal process reaches a low certain threshold, the aggregation of nanoparticles is restricted and microspheres are unable to form (Fig. S1).

TEM images of the three kinds of SnO2 microspheres in Fig. 2 could provide more information about the inner structures. It is proven again that the SnO2 microspheres are evenly distributed. In addition, it can be observed that spindly SnO2 nanocrystals developing from a center to the space radially to form the microsphere structure. The edge position of HS-C microsphere is further magnified as shown in Fig. 2d. From the HRTEM image, the lattice fringes have spacing of 0.34 nm, which shows a good coherence with the (110) planes of rutile SnO2. The inset of Fig. 2d shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of HS-C, the diffraction rings can be attributed to (110), (101) and (211) planes of rutile SnO2.

Fig. 3 a shows the XRD patterns of the SnO2 samples, which exhibit the same diffraction peaks assigned to rutile SnO2 (JCPDS 41–1445). There is not any impurity peak on the patterns. The intense diffraction peaks with broadened line widths mean the small crystallite sizes. By using Scherer equation, the average crystallite sizes can be obtained by estimating the full width at half maximum of the (200) peak, which are 22.0, 23.7 and 23.9 nm for HS-A, HS-B and HS-C respectively. Therefore, the homogenous SnO2 microspheres are constructed by the aggregation of tiny primary SnO2 nanocrystallites. Fig. 3b shows the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the homogeneous SnO2 microspheres. The isotherms display a typical type IV isotherm according to IUPAC classification, which is the characteristic of mesoporous materials. The BET surface area of HS-C is 70.9 m2 g−1, which is about 1.5 times larger than those of HS-A (42.8 m2 g−1) and HS-B (47.8 m2 g−1). And the average pore diameters of HS-A, HS-B, HS-C are 4.5 nm, 3.8 nm and 5.2 nm, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results further confirm that the products prepared from the divalent tin precursor are pure SnO2. Fig. 3c exhibits the typical broad-scan spectra of the homogeneous SnO2 microspheres with binding energy from 0 to 1200 eV, on which the primary peaks corresponding to O 1s and Sn 3d are marked. The high-resolution Sn 3d spectra in Fig. 3d shows two evident peaks at the binding energy equal to 487.1 and 495.5 eV, which is corresponding to the spin-orbital splitting photoelectron lines of Sn 3d5/2 and Sn 3d3/2 peaks separately [28].

Spin coating is the most widely used method to prepare the electron transport layers. Firstly, we prepared the SnO2 microsphere films by the conventional spin coating method. However, the as-prepared film is not fully covered and a large area of FTO substrate is exposed as shown in Fig. 4 a. The microspheres are easy to slip off from the FTO substrate due to the centrifugal force, and this problem had appeared in previous reports [14]. Therefore, a rather more suitable spray-coating method is adopted. Fig. 4b–d exhibit the surface and cross-sectional SEM images of the homogeneous SnO2 sphere films prepared by spray coating, from which the deposition of microspheres is much better than that of spin coating. Different spraying time of 10 s, 20 s, and 30 s (Fig. S4) has been investigated. For a short spraying time, there still exist plenty of exposed FTO substrates. With the extending of spraying time, the coverage of microspheres increases obviously. However, the microspheres will accumulate too thick if the spraying time lasts too long. The schematic diagrams of spray coating and the effect of spraying time on the surface morphology evolution are summarized in Fig. 4e. The microsphere colloids were loaded into the spray gun and continuously injected to the FTO glass on the hotplate with a tilt angle of 45° for 20 s. In particular, spraying with a small-angle is an established and interface sensitive scattering technique [29]. At the same time as the defects are reduced, the recombination of carriers is also reduced [23,25]. The film quality of the SnO2 microsphere electron transport layer has a key role in the photovoltaic performance of the PSCs device because of the susceptibility of the perovskite crystallization behavior to the interfacial carrier separation [30–32].

By using the spray coating method, the as-obtained SnO2 microsphere films show high transparency. Fig. 5 a demonstrates the transmittance spectra of SnO2 microspheres based ETLs. All microspheres based ETLs show high transmittance from 400 to 800 nm in the visible light region, indicating the smooth and homogeneous SnO2 microsphere films on FTO substrates. The HS-B sample gives a similar optical transmission as compared to the reference ETL, while the HS-A which has a slightly blurred appearance (see Fig. S3a) shows a relative lower transmittance. Meanwhile, the HS-C exhibits the highest transmittance, which is attributed to the smallest diameter and uniform spreading of HS-C on top of the FTO substrate as shown in the Fig. 4d. Fig. 5b exhibits the absorbance spectra of SnO2 microspheres based ETLs FTO/SnO2/perovskite. It can be seen that all samples show excellent light absorption capabilities over the entire visible light. And with the size decrease of SnO2 microsphere, the perovskites display increasing absorption intensity in the 550–750 nm wavelength range. The SnO2 microsphere based perovskites exhibit a better light absorption capability in comparison to the referenced one within optical region. Among all SnO2 microsphere samples, the HS-C with 75 nm diameter exhibits the strongest light absorbing capability. The absorption capability of the perovskite is enhanced as a result of the effective filling of (FAPbI3)0.85(MAPbBr3)0.15 perovskite into the SnO2 pores or interspace between SnO2 microspheres that developed from the uniform and aligned microsphere structure as well as the improved morphology and surface coverage in the perovskite absorber, similar to TiO2 nanorod [33].

Fig. 5c shows the surface morphology of the perovskite layer atop of the HS-C microsphere film. It is found that the perovskite has a good crystallinity and the grain size is as large as 1 μm. This is closely related to the increase of the perovskite contact sites provided by the flattened SnO2 layer. Therefore, high quality perovskite films are achieved. The structure of this device exhibits a good coherence with the latest perovskite solar cells, indicating good carrier mobility within this device. Fig. 5d gives a SEM sectional view of the completed perovskite solar cell, from which the SnO2-microsphere ETL distributes uniformly on the FTO substrate. The perovskite capping layer above the microspheres layer is about 500 nm. The perovskite seems to be uniformly infiltrated into the mesopores of the SnO2 microspheres layer. Additionally, the hole transport layer (Spiro-OMeTAD) can be distinguished clearly as well as the gold cathode layer.

The photovoltaic performance of PSC is characterized by J-V curves, IPCE spectra and EIS spectra. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the J-V curves are the best performing solar cells of each series, and the photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 1 . Obviously, the PSC from HS-C shows the highest PCE (16.85%) with the Jsc of 21.81 mA/cm2, Voc of 1.12 V and FF of 66.4%. With an increase of the microsphere size from 75 to 200 nm the Jsc does not change much, but the FF decreases significantly. Generally, the photocurrent mainly depends on light-harvesting and electron transport/collection inside the device. It can be seen that the HS-C with the smallest diameter and the largest specific surface area yields the best photovoltaic performance. Microsphere structure has a large specific surface area which helps to enlarge the contact area between SnO2 microsphere layer and perovskite. Therefore, the high injection efficiency of electron from perovskite to SnO2 microsphere layer is achieved. Meanwhile, the tight interconnection among the primary SnO2 nanocrystals makes charge transport through the SnO2 layer with high efficiency [34]. Hence, the recombination of electrons is decreased as well as the defect state. Compared with the SnO2 microsphere ETLs, the SnO2 nanoparticle PSCs show inferior photovoltaic performance with an average PCE of about 13%, which is comparable with the previously reported efficiencies [10]. The relatively lower efficiencies of the PSCs prepared from HS-A and HS-B microspheres could be attributed to relatively poor light absorption and much larger sphere sizes, leading to the low Jsc, Voc, FF and hence the inferior PCE. The statistical distribution of the photovoltaic parameters extracted from the J-V curves are summarized in Fig. 6 c-f, and the devices show a good repeatability [35]. Fig. 6b exhibits the incident photon-to-current efficiency (IPCE) spectra of the perovskite solar cells based on different SnO2 ETLs. The maximum IPCE value of HS-C PSC is as high as 89%. Obviously, as compared with the Ref-PSC, the SnO2 microsphere based perovskite solar cells have higher IPCE at light wavelength from 450 to 700 nm. Meanwhile, the HS-C sample exhibits enhanced IPCE values over the entire spectrum region.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) helps to understand the deeper insight of the electrical behavior in the devices [36]. Fig. 7 shows the Nyquist plots which are probed at open circuit voltage under AM1.5G illumination over the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–500 kHz, and the inset is the equivalent circuit with two parallel resistance-capacitor components in series. Two distinguished semicircles are observed on the Nyquist plots. The left semicircle close to the origin is the high frequency region corresponding to the charge transport resistance (Rct) while the right semicircle is the low frequency region corresponding to the charge recombination resistance (Rrec) occurring at the interface [37,38]. It is found that all the PSCs prepared from SnO2 microspheres exhibit much larger Rrec than that of nanoparticles, indicating the suppressed charge recombination at the SnO2 microspheres/perovskite interface [39]. The fitted Rct and Rrec values are summarized in Table 1. The HS-C PSC exhibits the smallest Rct and the largest Rrec as compared to other devices, which indicates the fastest electron extraction, smallest charge recombination rate and in turn the least interface potential loss [40,41]. Therefore, the excellent PCE of HS-C PSC can be attributed to the improved electron transport and suppressed charge recombination rate.

In addition, to further improve the energy level matching between the SnO2 microsphere ETL and the perovskite layer, an effective strategy is applied by adding graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to the SnO2 microspheres. According to previously study, the GQDs could improve the crystallinity of the perovskites and possess a tunable bandgap associated with the strong confinement and edge effects [42]. As a result, the GQDs modified HS-C PSCs yield the best PCE of 17.08%, with the Jsc of 22.5 mA cm−2, FF of 65.8% and a significantly increased open circuit voltage of 1.14 V (Fig. 8 ). The photogenerated electrons in GQDs could transport to the conduction band of SnO2, effectively filling the electron trap states as shown in Fig. S5a. This would lead to accumulation of free electrons and in turn improve the conductivity of SnO2:GQDs layer [43,44]. Meanwhile, a negative shift of the Fermi level for the SnO2:GQDs layer under illumination is beneficial for the improvement of Voc (Fig. S5b) [45,46]. Fig. S6 also compares the bandgaps of the SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs films, which are 3.75 and 3.81 eV, respectively. The SnO2:GQDs layer has a wider bandgap than that of SnO2 layer due to quantum size effect. In this case, the SnO2:GQDs layer is almost transparent to solar irradiation [17]. Eventually, this strategy leads to further improve the electron extraction and reduce the recombination. Consequently, GQDs modified SnO2 microsphere ETLs could successfully improve the photovoltaic performance of PSCs.

In summary, we exploited a facile solvothermal route to synthesize homogenous SnO2 microspheres with high specific surface area and good crystallinity. The diameters of the homogenous SnO2 microspheres are about 75, 110 and 200 nm, which are suitable for constructing the electron transport layers. Besides, the spraying method is exploited to prepare high quality electron transport layers. As a result, a power conversion efficiency of 16.85% is yielded by using the 75 nm sized SnO2 microspheres as the electron transport layers, which is attributed to fast electron transport and inhibited charge recombination. To further improve the charge transfer between the electron transport layer and perovskite layer, graphene quantum dots are added into the SnO2 microspheres, and a best efficiency of 17.08% is obtained. Consequently, this study demonstrates a great potential of homogenous SnO2 microspheres for the application as effective ETLs in PSCs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51572046, 21601121), the Shanghai Sailing Program (19YF1417600) and the foundation supported by State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials (KF1821). X. F. acknowledges the foundation supported by Shanghai University of Engineering Science Innovation Fund for Graduate Students.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2019.227405.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sequential deposition as a route to high-performance perovskite-sensitized solar cells
Nature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Burschka, N. Pellet, S.J. Moon, R. Humphry-Baker, P. Gao, M.K. Nazeeruddin, M. Gratzel, Sequential deposition as a route to high-performance perovskite-sensitized solar cells, Nature. 499 (2013) 316-319.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electron-hole diffusion lengths exceeding 1 micrometer in an organometal trihalide perovskite absorber
Science

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.D. Stranks, G.E. Eperon, G. Grancini, C. Menelaou, M.J.P. Alcocer, T. Leijtens, L.M. Herz, A. Petrozza, H.J. Snaith, Electron-hole diffusion lengths exceeding 1 micrometer in an organometal trihalide perovskite absorber, Science. 342 (2013) 341-344.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High charge carrier mobilities and lifetimes in organolead trihalide perovskites
Adv. Mater.
